original_abstract: In nature, non-heme iron enzymes utilize dioxygen to generate high-spin iron(IV)-oxo intermediates capable of performing a wide array of challenging oxygenation reactions. While synthetic chemists have spent decades attempting to replicate this reactivity, achieving enzyme-like activation of O₂ to form high-spin iron(IV)=O species has remained a significant challenge. Here, we describe a metal–organic framework containing iron(II) sites that closely mimic the local coordination environment found in α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases. Upon exposure to O₂ at low temperatures, the framework facilitates the formation of high-spin iron(IV)=O species, which we characterized through a combination of in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform (DRIFTS), variable-field Mössbauer, Fe Kβ X-ray emission, and nuclear resonance vibrational spectroscopies. We demonstrate that this framework is capable of the catalytic oxygenation of cyclohexane and the stoichiometric conversion of ethane to ethanol using O₂ as the terminal oxidant.
humanized_abstract: Non-heme iron enzymes utilizing dioxygen to produce high-spin iron(IV)-oxo species that catalyze numerous important and challenging oxidation reactions are among the most biologically and medically significant catalysts. Synthetic chemists have for decades attempted to mimic these enzymes and have thus far had limited success in mimicking enzyme-like activation and subsequent production of high-spin iron(IV)=O species. Here we present a MOF material bearing iron(II) centers that mimic the first coordination sphere of iron found in α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases. These materials allow for activation of O2 to high-spin iron(IV)=O at low temperature as monitored by in situ DRIFTS, variable-field Moessbauer spectroscopy, Fe Kβ XES and NRVS. In addition to being applicable catalysts in a heterogeneously catalyzed system of cyclohexane oxidation, the iron centers in this material can catalyze the homogeneous stoichiometric oxidation of ethane with O2 to produce ethanol.

These materials allow for activation of O2 to high-spin iron(IV)=O at low temperature as monitored by in situ DRIFTS, variable-field Moessbauer spectroscopy, Fe Kβ XES and NRVS. In addition to being applicable catalysts in a heterogeneously catalyzed system of cyclohexane oxidation, the iron centers in this material can catalyze the homogeneous stoichiometric oxidation of ethane with O2 to produce ethanol.
